title: Longest Arithmetic Subsequence
description: Find length of longest arithmetic subsequence

examples.explanation:
Start with empty subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=0.
Index 0 (3):
  Include 3: subsequence [3], diff=None (first element), length=1, max_len=1.
    Index 1 (6):
      Include 6: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=2.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [3,6,9], diff=3, length=3, max_len=3.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,6,9,12], diff=3, length=4, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,6,9], diff=3, length=3, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,6,12], diff=3? 6 to 12 diff=6 ≠ 3, not arithmetic, skip.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
      Skip 6: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [3,9], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,9,12], diff=6? 9 to 12 diff=3 ≠ 6, not arithmetic, skip.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,9], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,12], diff=9, length=2, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
  Skip 3: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
    Index 1 (6):
      Include 6: subsequence [6], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [6,9], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [6,9,12], diff=3, length=3, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [6,9], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [6], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [6,12], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [6], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
      Skip 6: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [9], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [9,12], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [9], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [12], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
Final max_len=4 from subsequence [3,6,9,12].

Start with empty subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=0.
Recursively explore all subsequences. Key paths:
Include 1: [1], length=1.
  Include 2: [1,2], diff=1, length=2.
    Include 3: [1,2,3], diff=1, length=3.
      Include 4: [1,2,3,4], diff=1, length=4.
        Include 5: [1,2,3,4,5], diff=1, length=5.
          Include 6: [1,2,3,4,5,6], diff=1, length=6, max_len=6.
Many other subsequences checked but shorter. Final max_len=6 from entire array.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsequences recursively and check if each is arithmetic, tracking the longest valid length. This approach explores all possibilities but is impractical for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Arithmetic Subsequence via Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that generates all subsequences and finds the longest arithmetic one.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Longest Arithmetic Subsequence problem using brute force recursion. The intuition is to explore every possible subsequence of the input array to find the longest one that forms an arithmetic sequence. An arithmetic subsequence maintains a constant difference between consecutive elements. We use recursion to generate all 2^n subsequences by making binary decisions at each index: include or exclude the element. For each generated subsequence, we check if it's arithmetic by verifying all consecutive differences are equal. We track the maximum length found. The algorithm starts with an empty subsequence and recursively builds it. At each step, we have two choices: include the current element (if it maintains arithmetic property) or skip it. When we include, we update the current difference and length. The base case is when we've processed all elements. Time complexity is O(2^n) because we generate all subsequences. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack depth. This connects to L1 by using the recursive generation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not properly tracking the current difference when starting new subsequences, and inefficiently checking arithmetic property for every subsequence. Real code pattern: define recursive function that takes index, current subsequence, and current difference; generate subsequences; check arithmetic condition; track max length.

Start with empty subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=0.
Index 0 (3):
  Include 3: subsequence [3], diff=None (first element), length=1, max_len=1.
    Index 1 (6):
      Include 6: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=2.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [3,6,9], diff=3, length=3, max_len=3.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,6,9,12], diff=3, length=4, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,6,9], diff=3, length=3, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,6,12], diff=3? 6 to 12 diff=6 ≠ 3, not arithmetic, skip.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,6], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
      Skip 6: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [3,9], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,9,12], diff=6? 9 to 12 diff=3 ≠ 6, not arithmetic, skip.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3,9], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [3,12], diff=9, length=2, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [3], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
  Skip 3: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.

        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [6,9], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [6,9,12], diff=3, length=3, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [6,9], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [6], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [6,12], diff=6, length=2, max_len=4.

      Skip 6: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
        Index 2 (9):
          Include 9: subsequence [9], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [9,12], diff=3, length=2, max_len=4.
              Skip 12: subsequence [9], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.
          Skip 9: subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=4.
            Index 3 (12):
              Include 12: subsequence [12], diff=None, length=1, max_len=4.

Final max_len=4 from subsequence [3,6,9,12].

Start with empty subsequence [], diff=None, length=0, max_len=0.
Recursively explore all subsequences. Key paths:
Include 1: [1], length=1.
  Include 2: [1,2], diff=1, length=2.
    Include 3: [1,2,3], diff=1, length=3.
      Include 4: [1,2,3,4], diff=1, length=4.
        Include 5: [1,2,3,4,5], diff=1, length=5.
          Include 6: [1,2,3,4,5,6], diff=1, length=6, max_len=6.
Many other subsequences checked but shorter. Final max_len=6 from entire array.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Subsequence Generation with Arithmetic Check
ladders.desc: Generate all subsequences recursively and check if each is arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible subsequences by making include/exclude decisions at each array index, and for each complete subsequence, verify if it forms an arithmetic sequence. We use depth-first recursion: at each index i, we have two branches—include nums[i] in the current subsequence or skip it. When including, we must check if adding this element maintains the arithmetic property (all consecutive differences equal). We track the current subsequence, its length, and the common difference. The algorithm: define recursive function generate(i, curr_seq, curr_diff, length). Base case: if i == n (end of array), check if curr_seq is arithmetic by verifying all differences equal curr_diff, update max length. Recursive case: branch 1—skip nums[i]: call generate(i+1, curr_seq, curr_diff, length). Branch 2—include nums[i]: if curr_seq is empty, add with diff=None; else if adding maintains arithmetic property (diff between last element and nums[i] equals curr_diff or curr_seq has only one element), update diff and length, then recurse. Time complexity: O(2^n * m) where m is subsequence length for checking arithmetic, but typically O(2^n). Space: O(n) for recursion stack and O(n) for curr_seq storage. This connects to L0 as the main algorithm, and to L2 which focuses on the arithmetic check logic. Common mistakes: not resetting diff when starting new subsequences, or incorrectly checking arithmetic property only at the end instead of during construction. Real code pattern: recursive DFS with backtracking to build subsequences.

examples.input: Array: [1, 3, 5], generate all subsequences and check arithmetic
examples.trace:
Start: i=0, curr_seq=[], diff=None, length=0.
i=0, element=1:
  Skip: recurse with i=1, curr_seq=[], diff=None, length=0.
  Include: curr_seq empty, so add 1, curr_seq=[1], diff=None, length=1, recurse i=1.
i=1, element=3:
  From skip path (curr_seq=[]):
    Skip: recurse i=2, curr_seq=[], diff=None, length=0.
    Include: add 3, curr_seq=[3], diff=None, length=1, recurse i=2.
  From include path (curr_seq=[1]):
    Skip: recurse i=2, curr_seq=[1], diff=None, length=1.
    Include: diff between 1 and 3 is 2, since curr_seq has one element, set diff=2, curr_seq=[1,3], length=2, recurse i=2.
i=2, element=5:
  Multiple paths, key arithmetic ones:
    Path: curr_seq=[1,3], diff=2, length=2.
      Include: diff between 3 and 5 is 2, matches curr_diff=2, so curr_seq=[1,3,5], length=3, arithmetic.
      Skip: curr_seq=[1,3], length=2.
    Other paths yield shorter subsequences.
After exploring all, max length=3 from [1,3,5].

Start i=0, curr_seq=[], diff=None.
Explore include/skip branches:
Key path: include 2 -> [2], length=1.
  include 4 -> [2,4], diff=2, length=2.
    include 6 -> [2,4,6], diff=2, length=3.
      include 8 -> [2,4,6,8], diff=2, length=4, arithmetic.
Other paths yield shorter sequences like [2,6] (not arithmetic), [4,8] (diff=4, length=2).
Max length=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking if a Sequence is Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Determine if a given sequence has constant difference between consecutive elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves verifying whether a sequence is arithmetic. An arithmetic sequence is defined as a sequence where the difference between any two consecutive terms is constant. The intuition is simple: compute the difference between the first two elements to establish the expected common difference, then check that all subsequent consecutive pairs have this same difference. Algorithm: given a sequence arr of length m, if m <= 2, it's always arithmetic (trivially true). For m > 2: compute diff = arr[1] - arr[0]. Then iterate i from 2 to m-1: if arr[i] - arr[i-1] != diff, return false. If all checks pass, return true. Time complexity: O(m) where m is sequence length. Space: O(1). This connects to L1 as the check performed on each generated subsequence, and to L3 which handles difference computation. Common mistakes: not handling sequences of length 0 or 1 correctly (they are arithmetic by definition), or off-by-one errors in the loop. Real code pattern: simple linear scan with early exit on mismatch.

examples.trace:
Length m=4 > 2.
Compute diff = arr[1] - arr[0] = 6 - 3 = 3.
Check i=2: arr[2] - arr[1] = 9 - 6 = 3, matches diff.
Check i=3: arr[3] - arr[2] = 12 - 9 = 3, matches diff.
All checks pass, so sequence is arithmetic.

Length m=5 > 2.
Compute diff = 4 - 1 = 3.
Check i=2: 7 - 4 = 3, matches.
Check i=3: 10 - 7 = 3, matches.
Check i=4: 14 - 10 = 4, does not match diff=3.
So sequence is not arithmetic.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Differences Between Consecutive Elements
ladders.desc: Calculate the difference between consecutive elements in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on computing the difference between consecutive elements in a sequence, which is fundamental for checking arithmetic property. The intuition is that in an arithmetic sequence, these differences must be constant. We need to compute arr[i] - arr[i-1] for all i from 1 to m-1, where m is the sequence length. Algorithm: given array arr of length m, create a list or iterate to compute differences. For i in range(1, m): diff = arr[i] - arr[i-1]. Store or use this diff for comparison. Time complexity: O(m). Space: O(1) if just comparing, or O(m) if storing all differences. This connects to L2 as the operation used in the arithmetic check, and to L4 which involves basic subtraction. Common mistakes: incorrect indexing leading to out-of-bounds errors, or not handling empty or single-element sequences. Real code pattern: loop from index 1 to end, compute and use differences.

examples.trace:
Length m=4.
i=1: diff = arr[1] - arr[0] = 10 - 5 = 5.
i=2: diff = arr[2] - arr[1] = 15 - 10 = 5.
i=3: diff = arr[3] - arr[2] = 20 - 15 = 5.
So differences are [5, 5, 5].

Length m=4.
i=1: diff = 5 - 2 = 3.
i=2: diff = 9 - 5 = 4.
i=3: diff = 14 - 9 = 5.
Differences are [3, 4, 5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Subtracting Two Numbers
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is simple subtraction of two numbers, which is used to compute differences between sequence elements. The intuition is that subtraction finds the difference between two values. Algorithm: given two numbers a and b, compute result = a - b. This is a fundamental arithmetic operation. Time and space complexity: O(1). This connects to L3 as the core computation for differences, and to L5 which involves understanding numbers. Common mistakes: incorrect order (e.g., computing b - a when a - b is needed), or overflow with large numbers (though in typical integer ranges, it's fine). Real code pattern: direct subtraction using the '-' operator.

examples.trace: Compute 10 - 3 = 7.
Compute 5 - 8 = -3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sequences and Elements
ladders.desc: Identify elements in a sequence and their positions.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves understanding what a sequence is and how to access its elements. A sequence is an ordered list of elements, such as an array or list. Each element has a position or index, starting from 0. The intuition is that we can refer to elements by their index to perform operations like subtraction. Algorithm: given a sequence arr, access element at index i as arr[i]. For example, in sequence [3, 6, 9], arr[0]=3, arr[1]=6, arr[2]=9. Time and space: O(1) for access. This connects to L4 as we need to access elements to subtract them. Common mistakes: using 1-based indexing incorrectly, or accessing out-of-bounds indices. Real code pattern: use square brackets with index to access elements.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with HashMap O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use DP where dp[i][diff] stores the length of arithmetic subsequence ending at index i with difference diff. Use hashmap for efficient diff lookup to build upon previous subsequences.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Arithmetic Subsequence with DP HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution using dynamic programming with hashmap to find longest arithmetic subsequence length.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this approach is that any arithmetic subsequence can be defined by its last element and the common difference. We use a 2D DP structure where dp[i] is a hashmap storing differences as keys and lengths as values. For each pair (j, i) where j < i, we calculate diff = nums[i] - nums[j]. The length of the arithmetic subsequence ending at i with difference diff is dp[j].get(diff, 1) + 1, where 1 is the base case (just the element itself). We take the maximum across all j for each diff at i. The overall answer is the maximum length found across all dp entries.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize dp as list of dictionaries, each dp[i] starts empty
2. Initialize answer = 1 (minimum subsequence length)
3. For i from 0 to n-1:
   a. For j from 0 to i-1:
      i. diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
      ii. length = dp[j].get(diff, 1) + 1
      iii. dp[i][diff] = max(dp[i].get(diff, 0), length)
      iv. answer = max(answer, dp[i][diff])
4. Return answer

Time complexity: O(n²) due to nested loops over all pairs.
Space complexity: O(n²) in worst case when all differences are unique.

Connection to L1: This builds upon the key subroutine of updating DP entries for each difference.

Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize answer to 1, not handling the base case properly when diff doesn't exist in dp[j], incorrectly calculating differences.

Real code pattern:
def longestArithSeqLength(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    dp = [{} for _ in range(n)]
    ans = 1
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i):
            diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
            length = dp[j].get(diff, 1) + 1
            dp[i][diff] = max(dp[i].get(diff, 0), length)
            ans = max(ans, dp[i][diff])
    return ans

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Update for Difference Pairs
ladders.desc: Update DP hashmap entries for all previous indices when processing current element.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine is the heart of the algorithm. For a fixed current index i, we iterate through all previous indices j < i, calculate the difference diff = nums[i] - nums[j], and update dp[i][diff] based on dp[j][diff]. The intuition is that if we have an arithmetic subsequence ending at j with difference diff, we can extend it to include nums[i]. The length becomes dp[j].get(diff, 1) + 1, where 1 represents the base case of starting a new subsequence. We take the maximum because there might be multiple j's giving the same diff for i.

Algorithm:
1. For current index i:
2. For each j from 0 to i-1:
   a. diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
   b. prev_length = dp[j].get(diff, 1)
   c. new_length = prev_length + 1
   d. dp[i][diff] = max(dp[i].get(diff, 0), new_length)

Time: O(n) for each i (worst case when i=n-1, j runs n-1 times)
Space: O(n) for storing dp[i] hashmap

Connection to L0: This subroutine is executed for each i in the main algorithm.

Common mistakes: Using wrong indices, not handling missing keys properly with .get(), incorrect difference calculation.

Real code pattern:
for j in range(i):
    diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
    length = dp[j].get(diff, 1) + 1
    dp[i][diff] = max(dp[i].get(diff, 0), length)

examples.trace:
i=3 (element 8), dp[0]={}, dp[1]={2:2}, dp[2]={2:3,4:2}
j=0: diff=8-2=6, prev_length=1, new_length=2, dp[3][6]=2
j=1: diff=8-4=4, prev_length=dp[1].get(4,1)=1, new_length=2, dp[3][4]=max(0,2)=2
j=2: diff=8-6=2, prev_length=dp[2].get(2,1)=3, new_length=4, dp[3][2]=max(0,4)=4
Also from j=2: diff=8-6=2 already processed, but we also check diff=8-4=4 from j=1 gives prev_length=dp[1].get(4,1)=1, but wait correction: Actually for j=1, diff=4, dp[1] doesn't have key 4, so prev_length=1, new_length=2, dp[3][4]=2
But from j=2: diff=8-6=2 gives length 4 as above
Final dp[3]={6:2,4:2,2:4}

ladders.title: Core Logic — Extending Arithmetic Sequences via Difference
ladders.desc: Understanding how to extend arithmetic sequences using common difference.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is that an arithmetic sequence is defined by its common difference. When we encounter a new element nums[i], we check all previous elements nums[j] to see if we can extend existing sequences. The difference diff = nums[i] - nums[j] tells us which sequence we're extending. If we already have a sequence ending at j with difference diff, then adding nums[i] creates a longer sequence ending at i with the same difference. The length increases by 1. If no such sequence exists at j, we start a new sequence of length 2 (nums[j], nums[i]). This logic works because arithmetic sequences maintain constant difference between consecutive elements.

Intuition: Think of building sequences backward from each element. For each pair (j,i), the difference determines which 'family' of sequences they belong to. Sequences with same difference can be chained together.

Algorithm concept:
For each pair (j,i):
1. Calculate diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
2. If sequence ending at j with diff exists: extend it to i
3. Otherwise: start new sequence of length 2

Time: O(1) per pair
Space: O(1) per pair

Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DP update subroutine.

Common mistakes: Confusing subsequence with subarray, not understanding that sequences can be non-contiguous, incorrect difference sign.

Real code pattern:
diff = nums[i] - nums[j]
if diff in dp[j]:
    length = dp[j][diff] + 1
else:
    length = 2

examples.trace: Sequence ending at 7 has diff=4. New element 11: diff=11-7=4 matches existing diff. So we extend: length increases from 2 to 3.

examples.input: Previous sequences: ending at 5: diff=2 length=3, ending at 8: diff=5 length=2. New element: 10
examples.trace:
Check pairs:
With element 5: diff=10-5=5, but sequence ending at 5 has diff=2, not 5. So cannot extend that sequence.
With element 8: diff=10-8=2, sequence ending at 8 has diff=5, not 2. So cannot extend.
Actually wait, we need to check if sequence ending at j has the SAME diff as nums[i]-nums[j].
For j=5: diff=10-5=5, check if dp[5] has key 5? No (it has diff=2). So cannot extend.
For j=8: diff=10-8=2, check if dp[8] has key 2? No (it has diff=5). So cannot extend.
Thus we start new sequences of length 2 with each pair: (5,10) diff=5, (8,10) diff=2.

testCases.input: Sequence at j: diff=3 length=4, nums[j]=10, nums[i]=13
Sequence at j: diff=-2 length=3, nums[j]=5, nums[i]=3
Sequence at j: diff=0 length=5, nums[j]=7, nums[i]=7
No sequence at j, nums[j]=1, nums[i]=4
Sequence at j: diff=10 length=2, nums[j]=20, nums[i]=25
Sequence at j: diff=7 length=3, nums[j]=15, nums[i]=22
Sequence at j: diff=1 length=6, nums[j]=100, nums[i]=101
Two sequences at j: diff=2 length=3 and diff=4 length=2, nums[j]=6, nums[i]=10
Sequence at j: diff=-5 length=4, nums[j]=0, nums[i]=-5
No sequence at j, nums[j]=50, nums[i]=30

ladders.title: Building Block — Storing Sequence Lengths by Difference in HashMap
ladders.desc: Using hashmap to store lengths of sequences keyed by their common difference.
ladders.explanation:
This building block involves creating a data structure where for each index i, we maintain a mapping from differences to lengths. The hashmap dp[i] stores key-value pairs where key is the common difference and value is the length of the longest arithmetic subsequence ending at index i with that difference. This allows O(1) lookup to check if a sequence with specific difference exists ending at i, and O(1) update to store new lengths.

Intuition: Different differences represent different 'families' of arithmetic sequences. At each position, we might be part of multiple sequences with different differences. The hashmap lets us track all these possibilities efficiently.

Operations:
1. Initialize: dp[i] = empty hashmap
2. Insert: dp[i][diff] = length
3. Lookup: dp[i].get(diff, default)
4. Update: dp[i][diff] = max(current, new_length)

Time: O(1) for each operation on average
Space: O(d) where d is number of unique differences for index i

Connection to L2: This data structure enables the core logic of extending sequences by difference.

Common mistakes: Using list instead of hashmap leading to O(n) lookups, not handling negative differences properly, memory overhead concerns.

Real code pattern:
dp = [{} for _ in range(n)]  # list of dictionaries
dp[i][diff] = length
current = dp[j].get(diff, 1)

testCases.input: dp[2]={2:4}, get length for diff=5 with default 1
dp[8]={1:3, 2:4}, get length for diff=3 with default 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Calculating Differences Between Array Elements
ladders.desc: Compute differences between pairs of elements in an array.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation involves subtracting one array element from another to get their difference. In arithmetic sequences, the difference between consecutive elements must be constant. For any pair of indices (j,i) where j < i, the difference diff = nums[i] - nums[j] represents the gap between these elements. This difference can be positive, negative, or zero. Zero difference means equal elements. The operation is mathematically simple but crucial for identifying potential arithmetic sequences.

Intuition: Differences tell us how elements relate numerically. If multiple pairs have the same difference, they could form an arithmetic sequence.

Algorithm:
For indices j and i:
diff = nums[i] - nums[j]

Time: O(1)
Space: O(1)

Connection to L3: These differences become keys in the hashmap storing sequence lengths.

Common mistakes: Reversing subtraction order (nums[j]-nums[i]), integer overflow with large numbers, floating-point issues if using non-integers.

Real code pattern:
diff = nums[i] - nums[j]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arithmetic Sequences and Differences
ladders.desc: Understanding what arithmetic sequences are and how they're defined by common difference.
ladders.explanation:
An arithmetic sequence (also called arithmetic progression) is a sequence of numbers where the difference between consecutive terms is constant. This constant is called the common difference. For example, in the sequence 2, 5, 8, 11, the common difference is 3 (5-2=3, 8-5=3, 11-8=3). The sequence can be increasing (positive difference), decreasing (negative difference), or constant (zero difference). Arithmetic sequences are fundamental in mathematics and appear in many real-world patterns like linear growth, evenly spaced points, or regular intervals.

Key properties:
1. Formula: a, a+d, a+2d, a+3d,... where a is first term, d is common difference
2. The nth term: a + (n-1)d
3. Any three terms a, b, c form arithmetic sequence if b-a = c-b

In our problem, we look for subsequences (not necessarily consecutive in original array) that form arithmetic sequences. A subsequence maintains order but can skip elements.

Examples:
- [1,3,5,7] is arithmetic with d=2
- [10,7,4,1] is arithmetic with d=-3
- [5,5,5] is arithmetic with d=0

Connection to L4: The common difference is calculated by subtracting elements.

Common misunderstandings: Confusing sequence with set (order matters), confusing subsequence with subarray (subsequence can skip elements), thinking differences must be positive.

examples.trace: Check differences: 7-4=3, 10-7=3, 13-10=3. All equal, so arithmetic.
Differences: 15-20=-5, 10-15=-5, 5-10=-5. All equal, so arithmetic.

approaches.name: DP with Optimized Space O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Similar to hashmap approach but uses array of hashmaps per index to reduce space by avoiding pre-allocation for all differences. Still quadratic time but more memory efficient.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Arithmetic Subsequence with Optimized Space DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP with array of hashmaps to find longest arithmetic subsequence length.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Longest Arithmetic Subsequence problem using dynamic programming with optimized space. The intuition is that for any arithmetic subsequence ending at index i with common difference d, we can extend it from a previous index j where nums[i] - nums[j] = d. We maintain an array dp where dp[i] is a hashmap mapping differences to lengths of arithmetic subsequences ending at index i. For each pair (j, i) with j < i, we compute difference d = nums[i] - nums[j]. We then look up the length of the subsequence ending at j with difference d (defaulting to 1 if not found), add 1 for including nums[i], and update dp[i][d] with the maximum length. The answer is the maximum length across all dp entries. This approach is O(n²) time and O(n²) space in worst case, but more memory efficient than pre-allocating a 2D array for all possible differences. It connects to L1 by implementing the complete algorithm using the key subroutine of updating DP entries. Common mistakes include not initializing default lengths correctly, forgetting to track the global maximum, or incorrectly handling negative differences. The pseudocode: initialize dp as array of empty dicts, ans = 1; for i from 0 to n-1: for j from 0 to i-1: d = nums[i]-nums[j]; length = dp[j].get(d, 1) + 1; dp[i][d] = max(dp[i].get(d, 0), length); ans = max(ans, dp[i][d]); return ans.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Updating DP Entries for a Pair of Indices
ladders.desc: The core function that updates DP hashmap for a given (j,i) pair with difference d.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP approach. For a given pair of indices j and i (with j < i) in the nums array, we compute the difference d = nums[i] - nums[j]. The intuition is that if there exists an arithmetic subsequence ending at index j with common difference d, we can extend it by including nums[i], increasing its length by 1. We use a hashmap dp[j] that stores, for each difference, the length of the longest arithmetic subsequence ending at j with that difference. We look up dp[j].get(d, 1) — if d exists in dp[j], we get its length; otherwise, we default to 1 (meaning a new subsequence starting at j). Then, we add 1 for nums[i] to get the new length. We update dp[i][d] to be the maximum of its current value (if any) and this new length. This ensures we track the longest possible subsequence ending at i with difference d. The time for this operation is O(1) average for hashmap lookups and updates, and it connects to L0 by being called for all O(n²) pairs. Common mistakes include incorrect default value (should be 1, not 0), not taking the max when updating dp[i][d], or miscomputing the difference. Pseudocode: d = nums[i] - nums[j]; length = dp[j].get(d, 1) + 1; dp[i][d] = max(dp[i].get(d, 0), length).

examples.trace: Given nums=[3,6,9], dp initialized as empty dicts for indices 0,1,2. For j=0, i=1: Compute d = nums[1]-nums[0] = 6-3 = 3. Look up dp[0].get(3,1): dp[0] is empty, so default to 1. length = 1 + 1 = 2. Update dp[1][3] = max(dp[1].get(3,0), 2) = max(0,2) = 2. So dp[1] becomes {3:2}. dp remains [{},{3:2},{}].

Given nums=[9,4,7], dp empty. For j=1, i=2: d = nums[2]-nums[1] = 7-4 = 3. dp[1].get(3,1): dp[1] is empty, default to 1. length = 1 + 1 = 2. dp[2][3] = max(dp[2].get(3,0), 2) = max(0,2) = 2. So dp[2] becomes {3:2}. dp is [{},{},{3:2}].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dynamic Programming with Difference as Key
ladders.desc: Using DP where state is (index, difference) to store longest subsequence length.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is to use dynamic programming where the state is defined by the ending index of a subsequence and its common difference. For each index i, we maintain a hashmap dp[i] that maps a difference d to the length of the longest arithmetic subsequence ending at i with that difference d. This allows us to efficiently extend subsequences: when considering a new element nums[i], for all previous indices j < i, we compute d = nums[i] - nums[j]. If we have a subsequence ending at j with difference d, we can extend it to i, increasing length by 1. Otherwise, we start a new subsequence of length 2 (j and i). By storing lengths keyed by difference, we avoid iterating over all possible differences and achieve O(n²) time. The space is O(n²) in worst case because in the worst scenario (e.g., all elements distinct and differences unique), each pair could create a unique difference entry. This connects to L1 by providing the overall DP framework that uses the update subroutine. Common mistakes include using a 2D array with fixed difference range (inefficient for large differences) or not realizing that dp[i] only needs to store differences for subsequences ending at i. Pseudocode: dp = [dict() for _ in range(n)]; for i in range(n): for j in range(i): d = nums[i]-nums[j]; length = dp[j].get(d,1)+1; dp[i][d] = max(dp[i].get(d,0), length).

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Pairs of Indices
ladders.desc: Generating all (j,i) pairs with j < i to compute differences.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves iterating over all ordered pairs of indices (j,i) in the array where j < i. This is fundamental because for dynamic programming, we need to consider how each new element nums[i] can extend subsequences ending at previous elements nums[j]. The intuition is that any arithmetic subsequence ending at i must have its second-to-last element at some j < i, so we must check all possible j. We use nested loops: outer loop over i from 0 to n-1, and inner loop over j from 0 to i-1. This generates O(n²) pairs, which drives the quadratic time complexity. For each pair, we compute the difference d = nums[i] - nums[j], which is the potential common difference for a subsequence including both elements. This connects to L2 by providing the pairs needed to update the DP hashmaps. Common mistakes include iterating j from i+1 (which would look forward, not backward), or missing pairs when i starts at 0 (no j). The pseudocode is straightforward: for i in range(n): for j in range(i): process pair (j,i). Time is O(n²), space O(1) for the iteration itself.

examples.trace: n=3. Outer loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: inner loop j from 0 to -1 (no iterations). i=1: j from 0 to 0, so j=0 → pair (0,1). i=2: j from 0 to 1, so j=0 → pair (0,2), j=1 → pair (1,2). Total pairs: (0,1), (0,2), (1,2).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Subtracting one number from another to find their arithmetic difference.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves computing the difference between two numbers, which is essential for identifying arithmetic sequences. Given two numbers a and b, their difference is d = b - a. In the context of the problem, if we have elements nums[j] and nums[i] with j < i, then d = nums[i] - nums[j] represents the common difference needed for an arithmetic subsequence that includes both. The intuition is that in an arithmetic sequence, the difference between consecutive terms is constant, so d tells us how much we add to nums[j] to get nums[i]. This operation is O(1) time and uses O(1) space. It connects to L3 by being applied to each pair (j,i) generated. Common mistakes include computing a - b instead of b - a (which would give negative of the correct difference), or not handling integer overflow (though not an issue in typical constraints). The pseudocode is simple: d = nums[i] - nums[j].

examples.trace: Given a=3, b=7. Compute d = b - a = 7 - 3 = 4. So the difference is 4.
Given a=10, b=5. d = b - a = 5 - 10 = -5. So the difference is -5, indicating a decreasing sequence.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arithmetic Sequences
ladders.desc: Basic definition and properties of arithmetic sequences.
ladders.explanation: An arithmetic sequence (or arithmetic progression) is a sequence of numbers such that the difference between consecutive terms is constant. This constant is called the common difference, denoted d. For example, in the sequence 2, 5, 8, 11, the common difference is 3 because 5-2=3, 8-5=3, etc. Formally, a sequence a₁, a₂, a₃, ... is arithmetic if aₖ₊₁ - aₖ = d for all k. The length of an arithmetic sequence is the number of terms it contains. A subsequence is a sequence that can be derived by deleting some elements without changing the order of the remaining elements. The problem asks for the longest arithmetic subsequence in a given array, meaning we want to find the longest sequence (by count of elements) that is arithmetic and whose elements appear in the same order as in the original array. This foundation is crucial because it defines what we are looking for: sequences where the difference between each pair of consecutive terms is the same. It connects to L4 by motivating why we compute differences between numbers. Common mistakes include confusing subsequence with subarray (subsequence allows gaps, subarray does not) or misunderstanding the common difference (it can be zero, positive, or negative). No pseudocode needed at this level, just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Check differences: 7-4=3, 10-7=3, 13-10=3. All differences equal 3, so it is arithmetic. There are 4 terms, so length is 4.
Check differences: 3-1=2, 6-3=3, 9-6=3. Differences are 2, 3, 3, not all equal, so it is not an arithmetic sequence.
